real_meaning: ～はず expresses the speaker's confident expectation or logical deduction — not a hope or wish, but a conclusion drawn from evidence, reasoning, or prior knowledge. The feeling is: 'based on what I know, this should be the case / this is what I'd expect.' It's the speaker staking a claim on reality based on grounds they believe are solid.

formation.rule: Attaches to the plain form of verbs, い-adjectives, な-adjective＋な, or noun＋の, followed by はず＋だ／です. Examples: <ruby>来<rt>く</rt></ruby>るはずだ (verb plain), <ruby>安<rt>やす</rt></ruby>いはずだ (い-adj), <ruby>静<rt>しず</rt></ruby>かなはずだ (な-adj), <ruby>学<rt>がく</rt></ruby><ruby>生<rt>せい</rt></ruby>のはずだ (noun). For past expectations use the past plain form: <ruby>来<rt>き</rt></ruby>たはずだ.
formation.common_mistake: Using the polite ます-form before はず. ❌ <ruby>来<rt>き</rt></ruby>ますはずだ → ✅ <ruby>来<rt>く</rt></ruby>るはずだ. Always use the plain (dictionary/short) form before はず.

sentences.jp: あの<ruby>店<rt>みせ</rt></ruby>、<ruby>今日<rt>きょう</rt></ruby>は<ruby>開<rt>あ</rt></ruby>いてるはずだよ。<ruby>月<rt>げつ</rt></ruby><ruby>曜<rt>よう</rt></ruby>は<ruby>休<rt>やす</rt></ruby>みじゃないから。
sentences.translation: That shop should be open today. Monday isn't their day off.
sentences.notes: The speaker gives their reasoning right after はず — this is very typical; はず almost always implies there's a logical basis, even if unstated.

sentences.jp: <ruby>田<rt>た</rt></ruby><ruby>中<rt>なか</rt></ruby>さんはもう<ruby>連<rt>れん</rt></ruby><ruby>絡<rt>らく</rt></ruby>を<ruby>受<rt>う</rt></ruby>けているはずです。
sentences.translation: Tanaka-san should already have been contacted.
sentences.notes: The speaker isn't guessing randomly — they're confident because they arranged it or have reason to believe so; はず marks this logical certainty.

sentences.jp: この<ruby>書<rt>しょ</rt></ruby><ruby>類<rt>るい</rt></ruby>は<ruby>先<rt>せん</rt></ruby><ruby>週<rt>しゅう</rt></ruby><ruby>中<rt>じゅう</rt></ruby>に<ruby>提<rt>てい</rt></ruby><ruby>出<rt>しゅつ</rt></ruby>されているはずでございますが、<ruby>確<rt>かく</rt></ruby><ruby>認<rt>にん</rt></ruby>いたします。
sentences.translation: These documents should have been submitted by last week, but I will confirm.
sentences.notes: Even in very formal speech, はず retains its 'I have grounds to believe this' nuance — the speaker is not uncertain, just being polite by offering to double-check.

sentences.jp: あんなに<ruby>練<rt>れん</rt></ruby><ruby>習<rt>しゅう</rt></ruby>したのに、うまくできないはずがない。
sentences.translation: After practicing that much, there's no way I can't do it well.
sentences.notes: はずがない is the tricky flip side — 'there's no logical basis for X to be true' — it's a strong logical denial, not just 'probably not.' Learners often miss this negative construction.

confused_with.contrast: Both express the speaker's prediction about something, but the basis and strength differ. はず is grounded in specific evidence or known facts — the speaker has a concrete reason. でしょう／だろう is more of a general probability or soft guess, often based on intuition or likelihood rather than hard facts. Minimal pair: <ruby>彼<rt>かれ</rt></ruby>はもう<ruby>家<rt>いえ</rt></ruby>にいるはずだ (I know he said he'd be home by now — logical deduction from something specific) vs. <ruby>彼<rt>かれ</rt></ruby>はもう<ruby>家<rt>いえ</rt></ruby>にいるだろう (He's probably home by now — a reasonable guess). Use はず when you can point to WHY you believe it; use だろう when it's more of a probabilistic feeling.

bunpro_tip: Mental model: think of はず as 'the expected outcome according to my internal script.' When reality matches your script, はず fits perfectly — and when it doesn't (e.g., <ruby>来<rt>く</rt></ruby>るはずだったのに…), that gap between expectation and reality is exactly what はず is built to express. Drill the negative はずがない and the past-expectation-violated pattern はずだったのに as early as possible on BunPro — they're where the nuance really shows.
